_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Grainger County, TN. Formed 1796 from Hawkins and Knox counties. Seat: Rutledge. No major courthouse fire or destruction. Records from 1796. Cherokee Lake (TVA) displaced some families; TVA archives supplement county records.

current_courthouse.location: Rutledge, TN — Main Street. One of the oldest active courthouses in Tennessee.

previous_courthouses.fate: Early log and frame courthouses. Records transferred to the current building.

record_losses.cause: None — no major fire or war destruction
record_losses.description: Grainger County's 1848 courthouse is one of the oldest still in use in Tennessee and has not experienced a catastrophic fire or wartime destruction. Records from the county's 1796 formation are well-preserved. The Civil War disrupted court operations but did not damage the building or its records.
record_losses.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name} lived in Grainger County after 1796, the records almost certainly survive in the same 1848 courthouse that served {pronoun_possessive} contemporaries. The building is a direct physical connection to the ancestor's legal life.
record_losses.source: Tennessee State Library and Archives. Guide to County Records: Grainger County.

deeds.repository: Grainger County Register of Deeds, Rutledge; TSLA microfilm

wills_estates.gaps: Earliest loose papers may be fragmentary.

court_minutes.completeness: Good — continuous with Civil War disruption

substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for tracking families. Agricultural schedules (1850-1880) document the county's farming economy in detail.
substitute_sources.source: NARA. Federal Census Schedules, Grainger County, TN.

substitute_sources.description: Cherokee Dam displaced Holston bottomland families from Grainger County. TVA land acquisition records document every tract.
substitute_sources.repository: Tennessee Valley Authority Technical Library; National Archives Southeast Region
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Documents displaced families — names, tract descriptions, compensation, and relocation patterns.
substitute_sources.source: Tennessee Valley Authority. Cherokee Reservoir: Report to the Congress. Knoxville: TVA, 1943.

substitute_sources.description: Baptist and Presbyterian congregations in Grainger County maintained records from the early nineteenth century. Church records supplement civil records, particularly for marriages before the 1838 state registration law.
substitute_sources.repository: Individual congregations; TSLA; Carson-Newman University archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Valuable for pre-1838 marriage documentation and for tracking families between census years.
substitute_sources.source: Carson-Newman University Special Collections finding aids.

narrative_hooks: Grainger County's 1848 courthouse is still in use — {ancestor_name}'s deeds and wills sit in the same building where they were filed, in a courthouse that has served continuously for almost two centuries.

If {ancestor_name}'s Holston bottomland was taken by TVA for Cherokee Lake, the condemnation records document exactly what was lost and what was paid — a paper trail in Knoxville for a life that ended in Grainger County.
Grainger County has no major record losses — if {ancestor_name}'s paper trail has gaps, look at the ancestor's circumstances, not the courthouse.
Tennessee required vital records registration in 1908, so the absence of a birth record for {ancestor_name} before that date is standard for the state, not specific to Grainger County.
source_references: Tennessee State Library and Archives. Guide to County Records: Grainger County.
Tennessee Valley Authority. Cherokee Reservoir: Report to the Congress. Knoxville: TVA, 1943.
Carson-Newman University Special Collections finding aids.
